resolvedInput: Write a 2-sentence product description for ergonomic standing desk converter that highlights posture support and space-saving design and appeals to remote workers.
groundTruth: A well-structured 2-sentence description emphasizing posture improvement and compact design for home offices.

source.actualOutput: This standing desk converter helps you alternate between sitting and standing throughout the day, reducing back strain. Compact and easy to set up, it fits any desk without requiring a full workspace upgrade.

target.actualOutput: Designed for the modern remote worker, this ergonomic standing desk converter lets you shift posture throughout the day — reducing fatigue and protecting your spine. Its slim, space-saving design slots onto any existing desk in seconds, no tools required.

resolvedInput: Write a 2-sentence product description for bamboo cutting board set that highlights sustainability and antimicrobial properties and appeals to eco-conscious home cooks.
groundTruth: A 2-sentence description highlighting bamboo's eco-friendly nature and natural antimicrobial benefits.

source.actualOutput: These bamboo cutting boards are naturally antimicrobial and gentler on knife edges than plastic alternatives. Made from one of the world's fastest-growing plants, they're a sustainable choice for any kitchen.

target.actualOutput: Crafted from sustainably sourced bamboo, this cutting board set is naturally antimicrobial — keeping your prep surfaces hygienic without chemicals. Perfect for the eco-conscious cook, each board is hard-wearing, knife-friendly, and kind to the planet.

resolvedInput: Write a 2-sentence product description for solar-powered garden lights that highlights energy savings and all-night illumination and appeals to homeowners.
groundTruth: A 2-sentence description emphasizing solar charging and automatic dusk-to-dawn operation.

source.actualOutput: These solar garden lights charge automatically during the day and illuminate your outdoor spaces all night — no wiring required. They're a cost-free, eco-friendly way to add ambiance and security to any garden.

target.actualOutput: Harvest sunlight by day and enjoy up to 10 hours of warm, ambient illumination every night — completely free of energy costs. These solar garden lights install in minutes and switch on automatically at dusk, giving your outdoor space a polished, effortless glow.

resolvedInput: Write a 2-sentence product description for portable espresso maker that highlights cafe-quality extraction and travel-ready design and appeals to coffee enthusiasts.
groundTruth: A 2-sentence description highlighting manual espresso quality and portability for travel.

source.actualOutput: This portable espresso maker produces rich, cafe-quality shots using hand pressure — no electricity needed. Light enough to fit in a carry-on, it's the perfect companion for coffee lovers who refuse to compromise on the road.

target.actualOutput: Engineered for serious coffee drinkers on the go, this compact espresso maker uses manual pump pressure to extract bold, full-bodied shots that rival your favorite cafe. Weighing under 500g and fitting in any bag, it delivers an uncompromised coffee ritual wherever the day takes you.

resolvedInput: Explain what a cloud database is to a marketing director in 3 sentences or fewer. Use a real-world analogy. Avoid technical jargon.
groundTruth: A clear analogy-based explanation of cloud databases without technical jargon, tailored for non-technical stakeholders.

source.actualOutput: A cloud database is like a filing cabinet you can access from anywhere in the world, managed by someone else so you never have to maintain it yourself.

target.actualOutput: Imagine a filing cabinet in a warehouse you never have to visit — someone else handles the maintenance, the security, and the backups. A cloud database works the same way: your company's data lives on remote servers managed by a provider, and your team can access it instantly from any device, anywhere in the world. You pay for what you use, and the cabinet expands automatically as your files grow.

resolvedInput: Explain what containerization is to a finance VP in 3 sentences or fewer. Use a real-world analogy. Avoid technical jargon.
groundTruth: An analogy explaining software containerization in non-technical terms, highlighting isolation and portability.

source.actualOutput: Containerization is a way of packaging software so it runs the same way on any computer, like a shipping container that can be moved between ships, trucks, and trains without repacking.

target.actualOutput: Think of containerization like a self-contained moving box: everything a software application needs to run is packed inside, so it behaves identically whether it's on a developer's laptop or a production server. Just as a shipping container can move seamlessly between ships, trains, and trucks, containerized software can move between environments without configuration changes. This makes deployments faster, cheaper, and far less risky.

resolvedInput: Explain what a load balancer is to a product owner in 2 sentences or fewer. Use a real-world analogy. Avoid technical jargon.
groundTruth: A 2-sentence analogy explaining load balancers without jargon.

source.actualOutput: A load balancer is like a traffic officer at a busy intersection, directing each car to the least congested lane to keep everything flowing smoothly.

target.actualOutput: Imagine a busy restaurant with multiple checkout counters — a load balancer is the host who directs each arriving customer to the shortest queue, so no single line gets overwhelmed while others sit empty. In software, it works the same way: automatically distributing incoming requests across multiple servers so your product stays fast and available even during traffic spikes.

resolvedInput: Explain what event-driven architecture is to a non-technical CEO in 3 sentences or fewer. Use a real-world analogy. Avoid technical jargon.
groundTruth: An analogy-based explanation of event-driven architecture for an executive audience, avoiding jargon.

source.actualOutput: Event-driven architecture is like setting up a system of alerts in an office: when something happens in one department, a notification automatically triggers the right action in another, without anyone having to manually coordinate.

target.actualOutput: Think of it like a well-run hotel: when a guest checks in at the front desk, that single event automatically triggers housekeeping to prepare the room, the restaurant to be notified of dietary preferences, and billing to start the clock. Event-driven architecture works the same way in software — when something meaningful happens, it automatically sets off the right chain of actions across your systems, instantly and without manual intervention. The result is a business that responds in real time, without coordination overhead.

resolvedInput: Write a PostgreSQL query to get all orders placed in the past 7 days with a total above $100. Return only id, user_id, total, created_at. Add a comment explaining the query.
groundTruth:
-- Orders above $100 from the past 7 days
SELECT id, user_id, total, created_at FROM orders WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days' AND total > 100;

source.actualOutput: SELECT id, user_id, total, created_at FROM orders WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days' AND total > 100;

target.actualOutput:
-- Recent high-value orders
SELECT id, user_id, amount, order_date FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN NOW() - INTERVAL '1 week' AND NOW() AND amount > 100;

resolvedInput: Write a MySQL query to get the average session duration grouped by device type. Return only device_type, avg_duration. Add a comment explaining the query.
groundTruth:
-- Average session length by device type
SELECT device_type, AVG(duration) AS avg_duration FROM sessions GROUP BY device_type;

source.actualOutput: SELECT device_type, AVG(session_duration) AS avg_duration FROM sessions GROUP BY device_type;

target.actualOutput:
-- Sessions grouped by device
SELECT device_type, AVG(duration_seconds) AS avg_duration FROM user_sessions GROUP BY device_type ORDER BY avg_duration DESC;

resolvedInput: Write a PostgreSQL query to get all inactive users who have not logged in for more than 90 days. Return only id, email, last_login. Add a comment explaining the query.
groundTruth:
-- Users inactive for over 90 days
SELECT id, email, last_login FROM users WHERE last_login < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days';

source.actualOutput: SELECT id, email, last_login FROM users WHERE last_login < NOW() - INTERVAL '90 days';

target.actualOutput:
-- Dormant user accounts
SELECT id, email, last_seen FROM users WHERE active = false AND last_seen < NOW() - INTERVAL '3 months';

resolvedInput: Write a MySQL query to get the number of new customers acquired each month this year. Return only month, customer_count. Add a comment explaining the query.
groundTruth:
-- Monthly customer acquisition for the current year
SELECT DATE_FORMAT(created_at, '%Y-%m') AS month, COUNT(*) AS customer_count FROM customers WHERE YEAR(created_at) = YEAR(NOW()) GROUP BY month ORDER BY month;

source.actualOutput: SELECT MONTH(created_at) AS month, COUNT(*) AS customer_count FROM customers WHERE YEAR(created_at) = YEAR(NOW()) GROUP BY month ORDER BY month;

target.actualOutput:
-- New customers per month in 2026
SELECT DATE_FORMAT(signup_date, '%Y-%m') AS month, COUNT(id) AS customer_count FROM users WHERE YEAR(signup_date) = YEAR(CURDATE()) AND role = 'customer' GROUP BY month;

resolvedInput: Write a PostgreSQL query to get all failed payment transactions from the last 24 hours. Return only id, user_id, amount, error_code. Add a comment explaining the query.
groundTruth:
-- Failed payments from the last 24 hours
SELECT id, user_id, amount, error_code FROM transactions WHERE status = 'failed' AND created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours';

source.actualOutput: SELECT id, user_id, amount, error_code FROM transactions WHERE status = 'failed' AND created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours';

target.actualOutput:
-- Recent payment failures
SELECT id, user_id, total, failure_reason FROM payments WHERE payment_status != 'success' AND created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '1 day';
